lead: A piano is one of the heaviest, most delicate and most cherished things in any home, and preparing one to be moved is not a job to take lightly. Get it right and the instrument arrives safe and sounding true; get it wrong and you risk damage to the piano, the property and the people lifting it. Drawing on years of handling pianos across Suffolk, here is how to prepare yours properly before moving day.

sections.body:
Pianos are unlike any other item in the house, and that is why they demand their own preparation. An upright can weigh a couple of hundred kilograms and a grand a good deal more, and that weight is distributed in a way that makes them top-heavy and prone to tipping. Inside is a tightly strung frame under enormous tension, thousands of moving parts, and finely fitted woodwork, all of which can be knocked out of true by rough handling, sudden jolts or careless lifting.

On top of the mechanical fragility, there is the finish and the value. Many pianos are beautiful pieces of furniture as well as instruments, with polished cases that scratch and dent easily, and many carry real sentimental worth, having been in a family for generations. A piano is rarely just an object to its owner, which is exactly why the preparation deserves proper care rather than a hopeful heave on the day.

In our experience, the moves that go wrong are almost always the ones where someone underestimated the piano and treated it like ordinary furniture. The preparation we describe here is about respecting what a piano actually is: an immensely heavy, delicate, valuable instrument that needs the right approach from the moment you decide to move it. Do the groundwork and the move itself becomes far safer and far calmer.

sections.h: Securing the Keys, Lid and Pedals
sections.body:
Before a piano travels anywhere, its vulnerable moving parts need to be secured. Start with the keyboard lid, or fallboard, closing it gently over the keys to protect them, and locking it if your piano has a lock. On no account force a stiff lid; if it does not close easily, leave it and let the movers manage it. The keys themselves are delicate and easily chipped, so keeping them covered during the move is essential.

The top lid of a grand should be closed and secured, and on a grand the music desk and any loose fittings should be noted so the movers can handle them. Pedals protrude awkwardly and are easily caught and bent, so they need protecting too, which our crews do as part of wrapping the instrument. The same goes for ornate legs, scroll-work and candle sconces on older pianos, all of which are fragile and stick out where they can knock against doorframes.

What you should not do is attempt to dismantle a piano yourself. The legs of a grand, the lyre that holds the pedals, and the action inside are all designed to be removed only by people who know exactly how the instrument goes back together. Trying to take parts off without that knowledge risks both damage and injury. Your job is to close and secure what closes easily; leave the rest to the specialists who will prepare it correctly.

Because a piano is so heavy and unwieldy, the route it will travel needs to be planned and cleared in advance, and this is something you can do before moving day. Walk the path the piano must take from its current spot to the vehicle, and again at the new home, and remove anything in the way, rugs that could slip, furniture, ornaments, low pictures and trailing cables. A clear, uncluttered path lets the crew move slowly and steadily without obstacles to dodge.

Measure the doorways, hallways and any turns the piano must pass through, and note any steps, thresholds or changes in floor level. Stairs deserve particular attention, as moving a piano up or down a staircase is the most demanding part of any piano move and needs the right equipment and enough people. If there are tight corners at the top or bottom of stairs, mention them when you book, because they shape how the job is tackled and how many crew are needed.

Think about the outside too. Where can the vehicle park, and how far is it from the door? Is the ground level, or is there a step, a gravel drive or a slope to negotiate? Are there gates or paths too narrow for the piano on its trolley? The more of this you can check and clear in advance, and the more you tell us when you book, the more smoothly and safely the move runs. A piano move is no place for surprises.

Pianos are sensitive to their environment, and a little thought about climate and timing helps the instrument settle well after the move. Pianos react to changes in temperature and humidity, which can affect the tuning and, over time, the woodwork. It is worth avoiding moving a piano in the most extreme weather if you can, and making sure the new room is reasonably stable in temperature rather than, say, right next to a radiator or a draughty external door.

On the question of tuning, it is best not to have the piano tuned just before a move. The journey and the change of environment will almost certainly knock it out of tune to some degree, so any pre-move tuning would be wasted. Instead, plan to have it tuned after the move, and after it has had time to settle into its new home, which we cover in more detail when we talk about aftercare. Mention to your tuner that the piano has recently been moved when you book them.

Timing the move itself sensibly also helps. Allow plenty of time on the day so nothing is rushed, since haste is the enemy of safe piano handling. If the piano is going into storage or a property that is not climate-stable for a while, factor that in, because prolonged exposure to damp or extreme temperatures is not good for any instrument. A piano rewards being moved thoughtfully and given a settled, sensible place at the other end.

The most important part of preparing a piano for moving is simply recognising that it is a specialist job and arranging the right help. When you book, give us full details, the type and make of piano, whether it is an upright or a grand, its rough age and condition, and the access at both ends including any stairs. This lets us bring the correct equipment, piano trolleys, skid boards, straps and padding, and enough trained crew to handle the weight safely.

On the day, your role is mainly to have everything ready and then to let the crew work. Make sure the route is clear, pets and children are out of the way, and the vehicle can get as close as possible. Point out anything we should know, then give the team space to do the heavy, careful work of wrapping, lifting and manoeuvring. A good piano crew works as a coordinated unit, communicating constantly, and the calmest moves are the ones where everyone trusts the process.

We will wrap and protect the instrument fully before it moves, secure it properly in the vehicle so it cannot shift in transit, and handle every threshold and turn deliberately. All our work is fully covered, which gives genuine peace of mind when you are moving something so valuable. Preparing a piano well is really about partnership: you do the groundwork of clearing, securing and informing, and we bring the equipment, skill and care to move it safely.

Sometimes a piano needs to go into storage rather than straight to a new home, perhaps because of a gap between completion dates, a renovation, or simply not having room for it just yet. Preparing a piano for storage involves the same careful securing and wrapping as a move, with some extra thought given to the conditions it will sit in. A piano does not like damp or wild swings in temperature, so a clean, dry, stable storage environment matters more for an instrument than for ordinary furniture.

Before it goes into storage, the piano should be wrapped to protect the finish from dust and knocks, with the keys and lid secured as for any move. It is sensible not to have it tuned beforehand, since you will want to tune it once it comes out and settles into its eventual home. If the piano is going to be stored for a long time, it is worth raising that with us so we can advise on keeping it in suitable conditions for the duration.

We've helped many Suffolk families store a piano safely during a move or a period of upheaval, then deliver it to its new home when the time was right. Because pianos need both specialist handling and careful conditions, it makes sense to arrange the move and the storage together with the same team. Mention it when you book and we will plan the whole thing, collection, wrapping, secure storage and final delivery, so your instrument is looked after from the first step to the last.

faqs: Can I move a piano myself with a few friends?

We'd strongly advise against it. Pianos are extremely heavy, top-heavy and delicate, and a slip can damage the instrument, the property or someone's back. They need specialist equipment and trained crew, particularly on stairs. Preparing the route and securing the lid is your job; the lifting is best left to people who do it regularly.
Should I have my piano tuned before it's moved?

No. The journey and the change of environment will knock it out of tune anyway, so a pre-move tuning would be wasted. Plan to have it tuned after the move, once it has settled into its new home. Let your tuner know it has recently been moved when you book them.
How do I prepare a piano before the movers arrive?

Gently close and lock the keyboard lid if you can, but never force it. Clear the route the piano will travel of rugs, furniture and obstacles at both ends, and measure doorways and note any stairs. Don't try to dismantle legs or pedals yourself, and tell us the details when you book so we bring the right equipment.
Can you store a piano during a move?

Yes. We can wrap and secure your piano and keep it in suitable, stable storage during a gap between homes or a renovation, then deliver it when you're ready. Because pianos need both specialist handling and careful conditions, it's best to arrange the move and storage together with the same team.
